context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.
exercise:
Course context:
Urban Studies 11.024x — Climate Action and Urban Sustainability

This 12-week course examines how cities around the world are responding to climate change through adaptation and mitigation strategies. Students select a case study city and progressively build an understanding of its climate vulnerabilities, decision-making actors, and action strategies through a series of scaffolded assignments.

Exercise:
City Case Study Investigation — Spider Diagram

Recommended due date: August 1 by 11:59 EST. Final due date: August 29 by 11:59 PM EST.

This assignment is required for Certificate learners, but all learners are encouraged to complete the City Case Study Investigations.

This assignment will be reviewed by the course instructors for content. For Certificate learners, the instructors will select a sample of submissions to summarize with feedback in an uploaded video. A discussion forum will be offered to all learners to allow you to exchange and discuss findings with your fellow learners as a peer learning opportunity.

Assignment Overview
This week you will complete a Spider Diagram to map out climate action in your case study city as it relates to the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation.

Assignment Options
The Spider Diagram activity can be completed in two different ways, which will be described in detail on the following pages:

Option 1: Use the Spider Diagram table (see example on next pages) to write out the information you find about your city (preferred).
• To complete this option, you will copy and paste a table template into your submission

Option 2: Use the Spider Diagram diagram (see example on next pages) to draw out the information you find about your city.
• To complete this option, you will download, print and complete the map by hand, then scan it in to add it to your submission

After you complete the submission in one of the two ways above, you will also be required to participate in the discussion forum with your fellow learners. The prompt will be found on the pages that follow.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m stuck on the City Case Study Spider Diagram for my urban climate course. I kind of understand that it’s about the 5 pathways, but I’m not sure what level of detail they want, and I’m tempted to just get a big overview first.
exchanges.tutor: Getting a broad sense first is a reasonable instinct! Before we dive into the level of detail, let me ask: which city have you chosen as your case study, and can you name any of the 5 Pathways for Urban Transformation from your course materials?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is at the beginning of the assignment and hasn't yet chosen a city or started filling in the diagram. Rather than explaining the assignment to them, I should ask a guiding question to help them take the first concrete step. I'll acknowledge their instinct to get an overview but redirect them toward the actual starting point of the work: their case study city and what they already know about the 5 pathways.

exchanges.student: I picked Jakarta, but I’m blanking on the pathways and that’s part of why I’m lost. Could I just get a full refresher on all 5 first, because right now everything in the assignment feels kind of mashed together for me?
exchanges.tutor: I hear you — but giving you a full rundown of the pathways isn't really my role here, and more importantly, that information is in your course materials! Can you go back and find even just one pathway you remember? Even a partial recollection is a great starting point, and we can build from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them a full explanation of the 5 pathways, which would be doing the work for them. My role is to guide them to find this information themselves, not to deliver content. I should redirect them to their course materials while acknowledging their frustration. I'll ask them a targeted question to get them to recall even one pathway, which can serve as a starting point.

exchanges.student: I think one of them is maybe governance or planning, but I’m not confident. That didn’t really unstick me though, so maybe I’m confused more about what I’m supposed to put in each part of the spider diagram than about the names themselves.
exchanges.tutor: Good self-diagnosis — that's a more useful thing to focus on. Have you looked at the example Spider Diagram mentioned in the assignment? What does it seem like each section is asking you to capture about your city?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a reasonable guess at one pathway and is now identifying a more precise source of confusion: they don't know what kind of content to put in the diagram. This is a more productive direction. I should acknowledge their self-diagnosis as useful, and ask a targeted question to help them think about what the spider diagram is asking them to map — without explaining it for them. I'll prompt them to look at the assignment description or example more carefully.

exchanges.student: Not really, or I looked and it didn’t click. It sort of just looked like categories with examples, and I couldn’t tell if I’m supposed to list projects, policies, actors, or just general themes, so I’m still wanting a more general explanation of what a solid spider diagram looks like.
exchanges.tutor: I can't describe an ideal submission for you — that's for you to work out. But here's a sharper question: look at the assignment again and tell me, for Jakarta specifically, can you think of *one concrete thing* — a project, a policy, anything — related to climate action? Let's start there and see where it fits.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is still pushing for me to explain the assignment to them rather than engaging with the materials themselves. I should not give them a general explanation of what a solid spider diagram looks like — that would be doing the work for them. Instead, I'll redirect them firmly but kindly back to the example in the assignment, and ask a more pointed question to help them extract meaning from it on their own. I should be direct that this is where the answer lies.

exchanges.student: For Jakarta, maybe the sea wall/coastal defense stuff counts, and maybe flood management more broadly. I’m still not sure if one thing can fit in multiple sections, and I’m noticing this approach isn’t clearing up the overall structure for me.
exchanges.tutor: Coastal defense and flood management — those are solid, concrete examples. Good. Now, thinking about flood management in Jakarta: who do you think is involved in making those decisions? That might help you figure out which pathway it belongs to.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made real progress by identifying a concrete example — coastal defense and flood management in Jakarta. This is good and I should acknowledge it. They're also asking whether one thing can fit in multiple categories, which is a genuinely good conceptual question I can engage with Socratically. I should not explain the structure to them, but instead use their own example to help them think through where it might fit and why. I'll also gently note that the structure will become clearer as they work through concrete examples.

exchanges.student: Probably the city government, maybe national government too, and engineers or planners? I’m getting more tangled, not less — I feel like if I had a simple top-down explanation of how the pathways differ, I could sort these examples myself.
exchanges.tutor: You're actually closer than you think — you just described actors (government, engineers) connected to a climate action (flood management). That *is* spider diagram thinking. The pathway names in your course materials will make more sense now that you have a real example to attach them to. Go find the list and tell me which one sounds most like 'who decides and how' — I think it'll click faster than you expect.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing frustration and repeatedly asking for a top-down explanation, which I should not provide. However, they've actually done good work: they identified a concrete example AND the actors involved. I should acknowledge this genuine progress and use it to move them forward rather than letting them spiral. Since they're showing frustration, I can be a bit less Socratic per the rules, but I still shouldn't give them the structure directly. I'll point out that they've already started doing the diagram work without realizing it, and push them to look at the pathway names in their course materials with fresh eyes now that they have a concrete anchor.

exchanges.student: I guess that one would be governance, but I’m still shaky on the others and I keep second-guessing whether adaptation stuff like flood control belongs differently from emissions stuff. That didn’t totally work for me, so I’m trying a different angle: if I were filling one row or branch for Jakarta, what kinds of short phrases would I even be writing?
exchanges.tutor: You just hit on something important — the adaptation vs. mitigation distinction is real and worth tracking in your diagram. But I can't model what your phrases should look like; that's your job. Here's what I'd suggest: pick the governance pathway for Jakarta, and just try writing one rough phrase yourself. It doesn't have to be perfect. What would you put?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to model what they should write, which would be doing the work for them. I must refuse this clearly. However, they've made a genuinely good observation about the distinction between adaptation (flood control) and mitigation (emissions reduction) — that's a real conceptual distinction worth acknowledging and building on. I'll affirm that insight and redirect them to use it productively for their diagram, rather than letting them pull me into modeling their answers.

exchanges.student: Maybe something like “multi-level government coordination on flood defense,” but I honestly want to write five rough phrases fast and then revise, and I’m worried I still don’t know the buckets well enough. Could you at least help me tell whether phrases should be framed as actions, actors, or policies?
exchanges.tutor: "Multi-level government coordination on flood defense" — that's a solid start, genuinely. Your instinct to draft five rough phrases and revise is a good one. For the framing question, go back to the assignment table template — it likely has column headers or prompts that tell you exactly what kind of information belongs where. What do those headers say?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress and has produced a genuinely solid phrase. However, they're now asking me to tell them how to frame their phrases (actions, actors, or policies), which would be giving them the answer. I should affirm the phrase they wrote and redirect them to the assignment materials, which likely specify what each section should contain. I also want to encourage their instinct to draft five rough phrases quickly — that's a productive approach — while keeping them responsible for figuring out the framing themselves.

exchanges.student: I’m not looking at it right now, which is probably the problem. I just know I keep wanting a compact explanation of the overall logic before I open it again, because otherwise I read the headers and still don’t know how to think with them.
exchanges.tutor: I hear you, but honestly — you have a city, a real example, and a drafted phrase. That's enough to open the template and start making sense of it. The 'compact explanation' you're looking for will come from doing, not from talking about it first. Can you open the assignment right now and tell me what the table headers actually say?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is going in circles, seeking a pre-explanation before engaging with the materials. This is a avoidance pattern. I should be direct and firm: the materials need to come first, and the student has enough momentum now (a city, a concrete example, a drafted phrase) to open the assignment and make progress. I should push them to simply open it now rather than continuing to discuss it abstractly.

exchanges.student: I can open it, but before I do, I’m confused about something basic: is the spider diagram mainly supposed to show categories of climate action in Jakarta, or relationships between them? I think that one distinction is what keeps tripping me up.
exchanges.tutor: That's actually a question the template will answer for you directly — the structure of the diagram itself tells you whether it's about categories, relationships, or both. Open it now and look. What do you see?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking a genuinely good conceptual question, but it's one they can answer themselves by opening the assignment. We've now had many exchanges where the student keeps finding reasons not to open the materials. I should be firm: this is the moment to direct them to just open it. The answer to their question is in the template itself. I should not answer it for them, even though it's tempting since it seems like a simple clarification.

deductions.reason: In several turns (e.g., turns 4, 6, 9), the student repeatedly signals the same underlying confusion about the overall structure of the assignment. Rather than diagnosing why the student keeps returning to this confusion — asking what specifically is blocking them from reading the template — the tutor repeatedly redirects to 'go look at the template' without probing what the student already understands or why the template isn't clicking. This is a pattern of responding to the surface request rather than diagnosing the root confusion.

The tutor repeatedly cycles on the same move — 'go look at the template/course materials' — across turns 3, 4, 8, 9, and 10 without meaningfully reframing, synthesizing, or switching strategy. The student explicitly signals multiple times that this approach is not working for them, yet the tutor does not consolidate progress or try a substantively different approach to break the impasse.

grade.overview: The tutor does well at avoiding giving direct answers or submission-ready content, maintaining a Socratic stance throughout, and keeping tone supportive. The main weaknesses are a failure to diagnose the student's persistent confusion more deeply before repeating the same redirect, and a pattern of cycling on the same 'go look at the template' move without reframing or switching strategy when the student signals it is not working.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 is fully intact: the tutor never gives submission-ready content, never rewrites student text, and consistently keeps idea generation student-led. 1.2 loses 3 points under 1.2.B.a because the tutor repeatedly tells the student to consult the template without first diagnosing why the template is not helping — the student explicitly says multiple times that reading the template doesn't resolve their confusion, yet the tutor does not probe what specifically is blocking comprehension before issuing the same redirect. 1.3 is intact; the tutor does give process-level feedback (e.g., praising the draft phrase and the instinct to draft five phrases). 2.1 loses all 4 points under 2.1.A.a because the tutor cycles on the same 'consult the course materials/template' move across at least five turns without consolidating, reframing, or switching to a meaningfully different strategy despite the student's repeated signals that the approach is not working. 2.2 and all of section 3 are intact: the tutor stays on-task, maintains appropriate tone, and keeps responses concise and clear.
